summary:
On the night of November 9, 1989, the Berlin Wall — the most potent symbol of the Cold War division of Europe — was opened, as crowds of East and West Germans gathered at the checkpoints and began crossing freely in both directions for the first time since [[event:2713|the wall's construction]] in August 1961. Within hours, jubilant crowds were dismantling the wall with hammers and pickaxes. The fall of the Berlin Wall marked the effective end of the [[event:113|Cold War]] in Europe and set in motion the unraveling of communist regimes across Eastern Europe and ultimately the [[event:2682|dissolution of the Soviet Union]] itself.

The immediate cause of the opening was a dramatic misunderstanding. East German Communist Party spokesman Günter Schabowski, reading a directive at a press conference, announced that East Germans would be permitted to cross the border, misreading the instructions to say the policy would take effect immediately rather than after a planned administrative process the next day. Crowds gathered at the checkpoints, and overwhelmed border guards — without clear orders to shoot — eventually opened the gates. The unplanned announcement had triggered an irreversible process.

The deeper causes lay in the accelerating collapse of East German communist authority. The [[ event:2726|Gorbachev reforms]] of glasnost and perestroika had signaled that Moscow would no longer use military force to prop up Eastern European communist governments, as it had in Hungary in 1956 and Czechoslovakia in 1968. Mass protests in East Germany, centered on Leipzig's Monday demonstrations, had drawn hundreds of thousands of participants by October 1989. Hundreds of thousands of East Germans had fled through Hungary, which had opened its border with Austria in May, rendering the Berlin Wall already largely symbolic.

The opening sparked [[event:2719|German reunification]] with remarkable speed. West German Chancellor Helmut Kohl seized the opportunity with a ten-point plan in November 1989. East Germany held free elections in March 1990, producing a government committed to rapid reunification. Under the Two Plus Four Treaty signed in September 1990, the four occupying powers — the United States, Soviet Union, United Kingdom, and France — gave up their residual rights over Germany. Germany was formally reunified on October 3, 1990, less than a year after the wall fell.

The fall of the Berlin Wall triggered a cascade of revolutions across Eastern Europe. The [[event:2729|Velvet Revolution]] in Czechoslovakia, the [[event:2730|Romanian Revolution]], and the peaceful transitions in Poland, Hungary, and Bulgaria transformed the political map of Europe within months. The Warsaw Pact dissolved in 1991, [[event:2682|the Soviet Union]] itself disintegrated in December 1991, and the Cold War order that had structured world politics for forty years came to an end.

The Berlin Wall's fall remains one of the most joyful moments of modern history — a night when the division of a city and a continent was suddenly, unexpectedly ended, and when peaceful popular pressure proved more powerful than the military might of a superpower. November 9 is now observed as German Unity Day, though the actual holiday is held on October 3. Fragments of the wall have been preserved in museums and public spaces around the world as symbols of freedom, resilience, and the power of popular will.
